subject: Mathematics
q: $\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x$, then $\sin x=$
question: $\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x$, then $\sin x=$
solution:
$\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})-\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x$
$\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}\right)-\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}-\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}{1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}} \cdot \sqrt{\cos \alpha}}=x$
$\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} \frac{1-\cos \alpha}{2 \sqrt{\cos \alpha}}=x \Rightarrow \tan x=\frac{1-\cos \alpha}{2 \sqrt{\cos \alpha}}$
OR $\cot x=\frac{2 \sqrt{\cos \alpha}}{1-\cos \alpha}$ or $\operatorname{cosec} x=\frac{1+\cos \alpha}{1-\cos \alpha}$
$\sin x=\frac{1-\cos \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha}=\frac{1-\left(1-2 \sin ^2 \alpha / 2\right)}{1+2 \cos ^2 \alpha / 2-1}$ or $\sin x=\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}$

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>cosec</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:

Given, cosec2tan-115+tan-134

=cosectan-12×151-125+tan-134

=cosectan-1512+tan-134

=cosectan-1512+341-512×34

=cosectan-15633=6556

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22}\end{array}\right): a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{22}\right\}$
Then the number of non-singular matrices in the set $S$ is :
question:
Let $S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22}\end{array}\right): a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{22}\right\}$
Then the number of non-singular matrices in the set $S$ is :
solution:
The matrices in the form
$$
\left[\begin{array}{ll}
a_{11} & a_{12} \\
a_{21} & a_{22}
\end{array}\right], a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{12} \text { are }
$$
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 1
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
2 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
At any place, $0 / 1 / 2$ means 0,1 or 2 will be the element at that place.

Hence there are total $27=3 \times 3+3 \times 3+3 \times 3$ ) matrices of the above form. Out of which the matrices which are singular are
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 \\
1 / 2 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{ll}
1 & 1 \\
1 & 1
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{ll}
2 & 2 \\
2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
Hence there are total $7(=3+2+1+1)$ singular matrices.

Therefore number of all non-singular matrices in the given form $=27-7=20$

subject: Mathematics
q: The integral $\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \cos \left(2 \cot ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}\right) d x$ is equal to
question: The integral $\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \cos \left(2 \cot ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}\right) d x$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & I=\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \cos \left(2 \cot ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}}\right) d x\right) \\ & \int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \cos \left(2\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1+x}}\right)\right) d x \\ & \left.\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \frac{1-\tan ^2\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1-x}}\right)}{1+\tan ^2\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1-x}}\right.}\right) d x\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \frac{1-\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)}{1+\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)} d x=\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \frac{-2 x}{2} d x \\ & =\int_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4}(-x) d x=-\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)_{1 / 4}^{3 / 4} \\ & =-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{9}{16}-\frac{1}{16}\right] \\ & =-\frac{1}{4}\end{aligned}$

q: Considering only the principal values of inverse functions, the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: Considering only the principal values of inverse functions, the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Given tan-12x+tan-13x=π4

⇒tan-12x+3x1-2x×3x=π4

Applying tangent on both sides,

⇒5x1-6x2=1 ∵tanπ4=1

⇒5x=1-6x2

⇒6x2+5x-1=0

⇒6x2+6x-x-1=0

⇒6x-1x+1=0

⇒x=-1, 16

But, since x≥0.

Hence, x=16

∴ A has only one element and thus, it is a singleton.

subject: Mathematics
q:
The value of $\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^2(2)}-1}{\tan (2)}\right)-\cot ^{-1}$
$\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}+1}{\tan \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}\right) \text { is equal to }$
question:
The value of $\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^2(2)}-1}{\tan (2)}\right)-\cot ^{-1}$
$\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}+1}{\tan \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}\right) \text { is equal to }$
solution:
$\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{|\sec 2|-1}{\tan 2}\right)-\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{\left|\sec \frac{1}{2}\right|+1}{\tan \frac{1}{2}}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & =\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{-1-\cos 2}{\sin 2}\right)-\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\cos \frac{1}{2}}{\sin \frac{1}{2}}\right) \\ & =\pi-\cot ^{-1}(\cot 1)-\cot ^{-1}\left(\cot \frac{1}{4}\right) \\ & =\pi-1-\frac{1}{4}=\pi-\frac{5}{4}\end{aligned}$

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given, sin-1sin2π3+cos-1cos7π6+tan-1tan3π4

Now simplifying each term we get, 

sin-1sin2π3=π-2π3=π3

cos-1cos7π6=2π-7π6=5π6

tan-1tan3π4=3π4-π=-π4

sin-1sin2π3+cos-1cos7π6+tan-1tan3π4

=π3+5π6-π4

=11π12

subject: Mathematics
q:
The sum of the infinite series
$\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{4}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{19}{4}\right)+$ $\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{39}{4}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{67}{4}\right)+\ldots .$ is :-
question:
The sum of the infinite series
$\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{4}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{19}{4}\right)+$ $\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{39}{4}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{67}{4}\right)+\ldots .$ is :-
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{4 \mathrm{n}^2+3}\right) \\ & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\left(\mathrm{n}+\frac{1}{2}\right)-\left(\mathrm{n}-\frac{1}{2}\right)}{1+\left(\mathrm{n}+\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\mathrm{n}-\frac{1}{2}\right)}\right) \\ & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\mathrm{n}+\frac{1}{2}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(\mathrm{n}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & \mathrm{T}_1+\mathrm{T}_2+\ldots+\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\mathrm{n}+\frac{1}{2}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & \mathrm{S}_{\infty}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$
option (4)

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>8</mn><mn>31</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is:
question: The sum of possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>8</mn><mn>31</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
Given tan-1x+1+cot-11x-1=tan-1831

⇒tan-1x+1+tan-1x-1=tan-1831,   ∴tan-1x=cot-11x, x>0

⇒tan-1x+1+x-11-x+1x-1=tan-1831

⇒tan-12x1-x+1x-1=tan-1831

Taking tangent both the sides we get, 

(x+1)+(x-1)1-x2-1=831

⇒2x2-x2=831

⇒4x2+31x-8=0

⇒x=-8,14

But, if x=14

tan-1x+1∈0,π2

and cot-11x-1∈π2,π

⇒LHS>π2 and RHS<π2 (Not possible)

Hence, x=-8=-324.

subject: Mathematics
q: If for some $\alpha, \beta ; \alpha \leq \beta, \alpha+\beta-8$ and $\sec ^2\left(\tan ^{-1} \alpha\right)+\operatorname{cosec}^2\left(\cot ^{-1} \beta\right)-36$, then $\alpha^2+\beta$ is_______.
question: If for some $\alpha, \beta ; \alpha \leq \beta, \alpha+\beta-8$ and $\sec ^2\left(\tan ^{-1} \alpha\right)+\operatorname{cosec}^2\left(\cot ^{-1} \beta\right)-36$, then $\alpha^2+\beta$ is_______.
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Let } \tan ^{-1} \alpha=A \Rightarrow \tan A=\alpha \\
& \cot ^{-1} \beta=B \Rightarrow \cot B=\beta \\
& \sec ^2 A+\operatorname{cosec}^2 B=36 \\
& \Rightarrow 1+\tan ^2 A+1+\cot ^2 B=36 \\
& \Rightarrow \alpha^2+\beta^2=34
\end{aligned}$

Also $\alpha+\beta=8$ (Given)
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore(\alpha+\beta)^2=34+2 \alpha \beta=64 \\
& \Rightarrow \alpha \beta=15
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha, \beta$ are roots of equation
$\begin{aligned}
& x^2-8 x+15=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(x-3)(x-5)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad x=3,5 \\
& \therefore \quad \alpha=3, \beta=5 \quad(\alpha < \beta) \\
& \therefore \quad \alpha^2+\beta=9+5=14
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The trigonometric equation $\sin ^{-1} x=2 \sin ^{-1}$ a has a solution for
question: The trigonometric equation $\sin ^{-1} x=2 \sin ^{-1}$ a has a solution for
solution:
$\sin ^{-1} x=2 \sin ^{-1} a$
$-\frac{\pi}{2} \leq \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x} \leq \frac{\pi}{2} ; \therefore-\frac{\pi}{2} \leq 2 \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{a} \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \quad-\frac{\pi}{4} \leq \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{a} \leq \frac{\pi}{4}$ or $\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \leq \mathrm{a} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\therefore|\mathrm{a}| \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\right.$ As $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}>\frac{1}{2}$ ). Out of given four option no one is absolutely correct but (c) could be taken into consideration. $\rightarrow|\mathrm{a}| \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ is correct, if $\mathrm{a} < \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ is taken as correct then it domain satisfy for $\mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ but equation is satisfied. $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}>\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}>\frac{1}{2}$

question: If y=∑k=16kcos-135coskx-45sinkx then dydx at x=0is
solution:
For, small values near 0, i.e., x<<..1

y=∑k=16kcos-1coskx·cosα-sinkx·sinα

=∑k=16kcos-1coskx+α
=∑k=16kkx+α

=∑k=16k2x+kα

 dydx=∑k=16k2=67136=91

subject: Mathematics
q:
Given that the inverse trigonometric function assumes principal values only. Let $x$, $y$ be any two real numbers in $[-1,1]$ such that $\cos ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=\alpha, \frac{-\pi}{2} \leq \alpha \leq \pi$.
Then, the minimum value of $x^2+y^2+2 x y \sin \alpha$ is
question:
Given that the inverse trigonometric function assumes principal values only. Let $x$, $y$ be any two real numbers in $[-1,1]$ such that $\cos ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=\alpha, \frac{-\pi}{2} \leq \alpha \leq \pi$.
Then, the minimum value of $x^2+y^2+2 x y \sin \alpha$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \cos ^{-1} x-\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\cos ^{-1} y\right)=\alpha \\ & \cos ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} y=\frac{\pi}{2}+\alpha \\ & \alpha \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right], \frac{\pi}{2}+\alpha \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right] \\ & \cos ^{-1}\left(x y-\sqrt{1-x^2} \sqrt{1-y^2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}+\alpha \\ & x y-\sqrt{1-x^2} \sqrt{1-y^2}=-\sin \alpha \\ & (x y+\sin \alpha)=\left(1-x^2\right)\left(1-y^2\right) \\ & x^2 y^2+2 x y \sin a+\sin ^2 a=1-x^2-y^2+x^2 y^2 \\ & x^2+y^2+2 x y \sin \alpha=1-\sin ^2 \alpha \\ & x^2+y^2+2 x y \sin \alpha=\cos ^2 \alpha \end{aligned}$
Min. value of $\cos ^2 \alpha=0$ At $\alpha=\frac{\pi}{2}$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is the sum of the first 10 terms of the series, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>13</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>21</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to :
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is the sum of the first 10 terms of the series, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>13</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>21</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to :
solution:
S=tan-113+tan-117+tan-1113+…… up to10 terms.

S=tan-12-11+1.2+tan-13-21+2.3+tan-14-31+3.4+……+tan-111-101+11.10

⇒S=tan-12-tan-11+tan-13-tan-12+……+tan-111-tan-110

⇒S=tan-111-tan-11

⇒S=tan-111-11+11·1=tan-11012

∴tan(S)=56

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>,Then a value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>,Then a value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is

=3tan-1 x

∴ y=3x-x31-3x2

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of solutions of $\tan^{-1} 4x + \tan^{-1} 6x = \frac{\pi}{6}$, where $-\frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}} < x < \frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}$, is equal to
question: The number of solutions of $\tan^{-1} 4x + \tan^{-1} 6x = \frac{\pi}{6}$, where $-\frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}} < x < \frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}$, is equal to
solution:
For $x \in \left(-\frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}\right)$, $24x^2 < 1$
So $\tan^{-1}(4x) + \tan^{-1}(6x) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{10x}{1-24x^2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6}$.
$\frac{10x}{1-24x^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow 24x^2 + 10\sqrt{3}x - 1 = 0$.
$x = \frac{-10\sqrt{3} \pm \sqrt{300+96}}{48} = \frac{-5\sqrt{3} \pm 3\sqrt{11}}{24}$.
$x_1 = \frac{-5\sqrt{3}+3\sqrt{11}}{24} \approx 0.054$ (valid, lies in range).
$x_2 = \frac{-5\sqrt{3}-3\sqrt{11}}{24} \approx -0.776$ (rejected, outside range).
Number of solutions $= 1$.

q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>⁡</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mfrac><mrow><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|" separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is equal to:
question: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>⁡</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mfrac><mrow><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|" separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is equal to:
solution:
Assume that ,
x2=cos⁡2θ;θ=12cos-1x2

∴tan- 1⁡1+cos⁡2θ+ 1-cos⁡2θ1+cos⁡2θ- 1-cos⁡2θ=tan- 1⁡2cos2θ+ 2sin2θ2cos2θ- 2sin2θ=tan- 1⁡cosθ+ sinθcosθ- sinθ
=tan-1⁡1+tan⁡θ1-tan⁡θ

=tan-1⁡tan⁡π4+θ

=π4+12cos-1⁡x2

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the maximum value of $\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)^{2}$ for $x \in\left[-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$ be $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}} \pi^{2}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$. Then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Let the maximum value of $\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)^{2}$ for $x \in\left[-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$ be $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}} \pi^{2}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$. Then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Let $\alpha = \sin^{-1}x$ and $\beta = \cos^{-1}x$. Using $\alpha + \beta = \frac{\pi}{2}$:
$f(x) = \alpha^2 + \beta^2 = \alpha^2 + (\frac{\pi}{2} - \alpha)^2 = 2\alpha^2 - \pi\alpha + \frac{\pi^2}{4}$
This equals $2(\alpha - \frac{\pi}{4})^2 + \frac{\pi^2}{8}$, which is minimized at $\alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$.
For $x \in [-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}]$, we have $\alpha \in [-\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{4}]$.
Distance from $-\frac{\pi}{3}$ to vertex: $\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{7\pi}{12}$. Distance from $\frac{\pi}{4}$ to vertex: 0.
Maximum occurs at $x = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ where $\alpha = -\frac{\pi}{3}$:
$f(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) = 2(\frac{\pi^2}{9}) + \frac{\pi^2}{3} + \frac{\pi^2}{4} = \pi^2(\frac{8 + 12 + 9}{36}) = \frac{29}{36}\pi^2$
Since $\gcd(29, 36) = 1$, we have $m = 29, n = 36$, so $m + n = 65$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Statement I: The equation $\left(\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^3+$ $\left(\cos ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^3-\mathrm{a} \pi^3=0$ has a solution for all $\mathrm{a} \geq \frac{1}{32}$.
Statement II: For any $\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$,
$\sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2}$ and $0 \leq\left(\sin ^{-1} x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^2 \leq \frac{9 \pi^2}{16}$
question:
Statement I: The equation $\left(\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^3+$ $\left(\cos ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^3-\mathrm{a} \pi^3=0$ has a solution for all $\mathrm{a} \geq \frac{1}{32}$.
Statement II: For any $\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$,
$\sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2}$ and $0 \leq\left(\sin ^{-1} x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^2 \leq \frac{9 \pi^2}{16}$
options: Both statements I and II are true.
Both statements I and II are false.
Statement $\mathrm{I}$ is true and statement $\mathrm{II}$ is false.
Statement $\mathrm{I}$ is false and statement II is true.
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\sin ^{-1} x \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right] \\
&\Rightarrow-\frac{3 \pi}{4} \leq\left(\sin ^{-1} x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \leq \frac{\pi}{4} \\
&0 \leq\left(\sin ^{-1} x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^2 \leq \frac{9}{16} \pi^2
\end{aligned}
$$
Statement II is true
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^3+\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)^3=a \pi^3 \\
\Rightarrow &\left(\sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x\right)\left[\left(\sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x\right)^2-\right.\\
&\left.3 \sin ^{-1} x \cos ^{-1} x\right]=a \pi^3 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{\pi^2}{4}-3 \sin ^{-1} x \cos ^{-1} x=2 a \pi^2 \\
\Rightarrow & \sin ^{-1} x\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{\pi^2}{12}(1-8 a) \\
\Rightarrow &\left(\sin ^{-1} x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^2=\frac{\pi^2}{12}(8 a-1)+\frac{\pi^2}{16} \\
\Rightarrow &\left(\sin ^{-1} x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^2=\frac{\pi^2}{48}(32 a-1)
\end{aligned}
$$
Putting this value in equation (1)
$$
0 \leq \frac{\pi^2}{48}(32 a-1) \leq \frac{9}{16} \pi^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow 0 \leq 32 a-1 \leq 27 \\
&\frac{1}{32} \leq a \leq \frac{7}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$
Statement-I is also true.

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>cos</mi><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to :
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mi>cos</mi><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to :
solution:
Given cos-1x-cos-1y2=α

cos-1A-cos-1B=cos-1AB+1-A21-B2,

⇒cos-1xy2+1-x2×1-y24=α

⇒xy2+1-x2×1-y24=cosα

⇒cosα-xy2=1-x2×1-y24

Squaring both sides, we get
cos2α+x2y24-xycosα=1-x21-y24

⇒cos2α+x2y24-xycosα=1-x2-y24+x2y24

⇒x2-xycosα+y24=1-cos2α

⇒x2-xycosα+y24=sin2α

⇒4x2-4xycosα+y2=4sin2α.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the sum of all the solutions of
tan-12x1-x2+cot-11-x22x=π3, -1<x<1,x≠0, is α-43, then α is equal to _____ .
question:
If the sum of all the solutions of
tan-12x1-x2+cot-11-x22x=π3, -1<x<1,x≠0, is α-43, then α is equal to _____ .
solution:
Given,

tan-12x1-x2+cot-11-x22x=π3 where x∈-1,1

Case 1: When x<0, then

tan-12x1-x2+cot-11-x22x=π3

⇒2tan-1x+π+2tan-1x=π3

⇒4tan-1x=-π+π3

⇒4tan-1x=-2π3

⇒tan-1x=-π6

⇒x=-13

Case 2: When x≥0,

tan-12x1-x2+cot-11-x22x=π3

⇒2tan-1x+2tan-1x=π3

⇒tan-1x=π12

⇒x=2-3

So, sum of solution will be 

-13+2-3=2-43

Now, on comparing with α-43, we get

α=2

subject: Mathematics
q: $\cos \left(\sin ^{-1} \frac{3}{5}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{5}{13}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{33}{65}\right)$ is equal to:
question: $\cos \left(\sin ^{-1} \frac{3}{5}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{5}{13}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{33}{65}\right)$ is equal to:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \cos \left(\sin ^{-1} \frac{3}{5}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{5}{13}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{33}{65}\right) \\ & \cos \left(\tan ^{-1} \frac{3}{4}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{5}{12}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{33}{56}\right) \\ & \cos \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{3}{4}+\frac{5}{12}}{1+\frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{12}}\right)+\tan ^{-1} \frac{33}{56}\right) \\ & \cos \left(\tan ^{-1} \frac{56}{33}+\cot ^{-1} \frac{56}{33}\right) \\ & \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $0 < \alpha < 1$, $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3\alpha}$ and $\tan^{-1}(1-\alpha) + \tan^{-1}(1-\beta) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$. Then $6(\alpha + \beta)$ is equal to:
question: Let $0 < \alpha < 1$, $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3\alpha}$ and $\tan^{-1}(1-\alpha) + \tan^{-1}(1-\beta) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$. Then $6(\alpha + \beta)$ is equal to:
solution:
Given $\tan^{-1}(1-\alpha) + \tan^{-1}(1-\beta) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

Applying the formula $\tan^{-1}x + \tan^{-1}y = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+y}{1-xy}\right)$, we get:

$\dfrac{(1-\alpha) + (1-\beta)}{1 - (1-\alpha)(1-\beta)} = \tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = 1$

$2 - \alpha - \beta = 1 - (1 - \alpha - \beta + \alpha\beta)$

$2 - \alpha - \beta = \alpha + \beta - \alpha\beta$

$2(\alpha + \beta) = 2 + \alpha\beta$

Given $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3\alpha}$, we have $\alpha\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$. Substituting this into the equation:

$2(\alpha + \beta) = 2 + \dfrac{1}{3}$

$2(\alpha + \beta) = \dfrac{7}{3}$

$\alpha + \beta = \dfrac{7}{6}$

Therefore, $6(\alpha + \beta) = 6 \times \dfrac{7}{6} = 7$

Answer: $7$

q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cot</mi><mfenced><mrow><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>50</mn></munderover><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cot</mi><mfenced><mrow><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>50</mn></munderover><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Now ∑n=150tan-111+n+n2=∑n=150tan-1n+1-n1+nn+1

=∑n=150tan-1n+1-tan-1n

=tan-151-tan-11=tan-151-11+51

=tan-15052=cot-12625

So cot∑n=150tan-111+n+n2=cotcot-12625=2625

subject: Mathematics
q: For $n \in \mathrm{N}$, if $\cot ^{-1} 3+\cot ^{-1} 4+\cot ^{-1} 5+\cot ^{-1} n=\frac{\pi}{4}$, then $n$ is equal to_____
question: For $n \in \mathrm{N}$, if $\cot ^{-1} 3+\cot ^{-1} 4+\cot ^{-1} 5+\cot ^{-1} n=\frac{\pi}{4}$, then $n$ is equal to_____
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \cot ^{-1} 3+\cot ^{-1} 4+\cot ^{-1} 5+\cot ^1 n=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{4}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{5}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{46}{48}\right)+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{23}{24}\right)+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}=\tan ^{-1} 1-\tan ^{-1} \frac{23}{24}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{n}=\tan ^{-1} 1-\tan ^{-1} \frac{23}{24} \\ & \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{n}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\frac{23}{24}}{1+\frac{23}{24}}\right) \\ & \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{n}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{24}}{\frac{47}{24}}\right) \\ & \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{n}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{47} \\ & n=47\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $[\cdot]$ denote the greatest integer function. If the domain of the function $f(x) = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+[x]}{3}\right)$ is $[\alpha, \beta)$, then $\alpha^2 + \beta^2$ is equal to:
question: Let $[\cdot]$ denote the greatest integer function. If the domain of the function $f(x) = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+[x]}{3}\right)$ is $[\alpha, \beta)$, then $\alpha^2 + \beta^2$ is equal to:
solution:
For the function $f(x) = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+[x]}{3}\right)$ to be defined, the argument of the inverse sine function must lie in the interval $[-1, 1]$.

$-1 \le \dfrac{x+[x]}{3} \le 1$

$-3 \le x + [x] \le 3$

Using the fractional part function $\{x\}$, we can write $x = [x] + \{x\}$, where $0 \le \{x\} < 1$. Substituting this into the inequality gives:

$-3 \le 2[x] + \{x\} \le 3$

We analyze the possible integer values for $[x]$:

If $[x] \le -2$, then $2[x] + \{x\} \le -4 + \{x\} < -3$, which does not satisfy the inequality.

If $[x] = -1$, then $2[x] + \{x\} = -2 + \{x\}$. Since $0 \le \{x\} < 1$, we have $-2 \le -2 + \{x\} < -1$, which satisfies the inequality. This gives $x \in [-1, 0)$.

If $[x] = 0$, then $2[x] + \{x\} = \{x\}$. Since $0 \le \{x\} < 1$, this satisfies the inequality. This gives $x \in [0, 1)$.

If $[x] = 1$, then $2[x] + \{x\} = 2 + \{x\}$. Since $0 \le \{x\} < 1$, we have $2 \le 2 + \{x\} < 3$, which satisfies the inequality. This gives $x \in [1, 2)$.

If $[x] \ge 2$, then $2[x] + \{x\} \ge 4 + \{x\} \ge 4 > 3$, which does not satisfy the inequality.

Taking the union of the valid intervals, the domain of $f(x)$ is $[-1, 0) \cup [0, 1) \cup [1, 2) = [-1, 2)$.

Comparing this with the given domain $[\alpha, \beta)$, we get $\alpha = -1$ and $\beta = 2$.

Therefore, $\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = (-1)^2 + (2)^2 = 1 + 4 = 5$.

Answer: $5$

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>17</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfrac><mn>77</mn><mn>36</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>α</mi><mo><</mo><mn>13</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>17</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfrac><mn>77</mn><mn>36</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>α</mi><mo><</mo><mn>13</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

sin-1α17+cos-145-tan-17736=0

⇒sin-1α17=tan-17736-cos-145

⇒sin-1α17=tan-17736-tan-134

⇒sin-1α17=tan-17736-341+7736×34

⇒sin-1α17=tan-1815

⇒sin-1α17=sin-1817

⇒α=8

Now solving sin-1sinα+cos-1cosα=sin-1sinα+cos-1cosα

⇒sin-1sin8+cos-1cos8=3π-8+8-2π=π

subject: Mathematics
q: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>π</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> equal to
question: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>π</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>−</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> equal to
solution:
Given: sin−1α+sin−1β+sin−1γ=π

Let, sin−1α=A, sin−1β=B, sin−1γ=C

⇒A+B+C=π

Also, α=sinA, β=sinB, γ=sinC   ...i

It is given that, α+β2−γ2=3αβ.

⇒α2+β2−γ2=αβ

⇒sin2A+sin2B-sin2C=sinAsinB

⇒sin2A+sinB+CsinB-C=sinAsinB

⇒sin2A+sinπ-AsinB-C=sinAsinB

⇒sin2A+sinAsinB-C=sinAsinB

⇒sinAsinA+sinB-C=sinAsinB

⇒sinAsinA+sinB-C-sinAsinB=0

⇒sinAsinA+sinB-C-sinB=0

⇒sinAsinB+C+sinB-C-sinB=0

⇒sinA2sinBcosC-sinB=0

⇒sinAsinB2cosC-1=0

⇒sinA=0 or sinB=0 or cosC=12

It is given that, α,β,γ≠0

⇒cosC=12

⇒sinC=32

⇒γ=32

subject: Mathematics
q: The value of <math><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></math> is equal to:
question: The value of <math><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></math> is equal to:
solution:
sin-1⁡1213-sin-1⁡35
=tan-1⁡125-tan-1⁡34  sin-1⁡x=tan-1⁡x1-x2
=tan-1⁡125-341+12×35×4
=tan-1⁡3356
=sin-1⁡3365
=cos-1⁡5665=π2-sin-1⁡5665
